diseases.name: Leaf Spot / Early Blight
symptoms: Dark brown or black circular spots on leaves with yellow halo
Spots have concentric rings (target-board pattern)
Starts on older lower leaves first
Leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
Fruit may develop dark sunken lesions
diseases.cause: Fungal infection by Alternaria solani. Spreads through water splash and wind.
diseases.favourable_conditions: High humidity above 90%, temperatures 24-29 degrees C, wet weather or overhead irrigation

organic_remedies.name: Neem Oil Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 50ml cold-pressed neem oil plus 5 drops liquid soap in 5 litres of water. Shake well before use.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Apply in early morning or evening to avoid leaf burn.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 7 days for 4-5 weeks. Start at first sign of disease.

organic_remedies.name: Trichoderma Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 250g Trichoderma viride powder in 15 litres of water.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on leaves or drench soil around plant base.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 15 days as prevention; every 7 days for active infection.

organic_remedies.name: Bordeaux Mixture
organic_remedies.preparation: Dissolve 100g copper sulphate in 5L water separately. Dissolve 100g lime in another 5L water. Mix both slowly. Should be sky-blue colour.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on leaves. Do not apply during flowering as it may reduce fruit set.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 10-15 days.

prevention: Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately — do not compost them
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead watering
Maintain good spacing between plants for air circulation
Rotate crops every season — do not plant tomato/potato in the same spot two years in a row
Apply organic mulch to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves

faqs.q: Can I eat tomatoes from an infected plant?
faqs.a: Yes, the fruit is safe to eat if it has no visible lesions. Remove and discard any fruit with dark sunken spots.

faqs.q: Will the spots spread to other plants?
faqs.a: Yes, spores spread through water splash and wind. Spray immediately and remove infected leaves.

faqs.q: Is it different from late blight?
faqs.a: Yes. Early blight spots are dry and brown with rings. Late blight spots are water-soaked and have white fuzzy mold underneath.

faqs.q: How long does neem oil treatment take to show results?
faqs.a: You should see reduced new spot formation in 7-10 days. Existing spots will not disappear but new spread stops.

faqs.q: What if I don't have neem oil?
faqs.a: Use wood ash dissolved in water, or cow urine diluted 1:10 with water, or garlic-chilli spray as alternatives.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: If more than 30% of leaves are affected within one week, or if the disease spreads to the fruit.

diseases.name: Late Blight
symptoms: Water-soaked pale green or grey-brown lesions on leaves
White fuzzy mold on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Lesions spread very rapidly — can destroy field in 3-5 days
Fruit develops firm brown rot with rough texture
Strong unpleasant smell from infected tissue
diseases.cause: Oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans. Very aggressive — spreads fast in cool wet weather.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Cool temperatures 10-25 degrees C, rain or heavy dew, high humidity above 90%

organic_remedies.name: Copper Oxychloride Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Dissolve 300g copper oxychloride in 15 litres of water.
organic_remedies.application: Spray entire plant thoroughly including undersides of leaves.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 5-7 days during rainy season. This is the most effective organic option.

organic_remedies.name: Panchagavya Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 300ml Panchagavya in 10 litres of water (3% solution).
organic_remedies.application: Spray on leaves and soil around plant.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 10 days as prevention; every 5 days for active infection.

prevention: Do not work in the field when leaves are wet — you spread spores on your clothes
Destroy all infected plant material by burning — do not leave in field
Do not store infected potatoes near healthy plants
Plant disease-resistant varieties in future seasons

faqs.q: Is late blight worse than early blight?
faqs.a: Yes. Late blight is much more aggressive and can destroy an entire field within a week. Act immediately if you see white fuzzy mold.

faqs.q: Can late blight spread from potato to tomato?
faqs.a: Yes, the same pathogen affects both. Keep potatoes and tomatoes away from each other.

faqs.q: My plant is already severely infected — can it recover?
faqs.a: Severely infected plants rarely recover. Remove and burn them to protect the rest of the field.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: Immediately if you see white fuzzy mold on leaf undersides. This disease can destroy entire crop in days.

diseases.name: Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
symptoms: Leaves curl upward and turn yellow, especially young leaves
Plants are stunted and bushy with shortened internodes
Flowers drop before fruit sets
Young leaves are much smaller than normal
Tiny white whitefly insects visible on leaf undersides
diseases.cause: Begomovirus spread by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). The virus itself has no cure — must control the whitefly vector.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Dry hot weather with large whitefly populations, drought conditions

organic_remedies.name: Yellow Sticky Traps
organic_remedies.preparation: Buy or make yellow boards coated with sticky grease or castor oil.
organic_remedies.application: Hang at crop canopy level. Use 10-15 traps per acre.
organic_remedies.frequency: Replace every 2-3 weeks or when full.

organic_remedies.name: Neem Seed Kernel Extract (NSKE 5%)
organic_remedies.preparation: Soak 500g neem seeds overnight. Crush and filter through cloth. Add to 10 litres of water.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on both leaf surfaces, especially undersides where whiteflies live.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 5 days for 4 applications to break whitefly life cycle.

organic_remedies.name: Soap and Water Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Dissolve 10g bar soap (not detergent) in 1 litre of water.
organic_remedies.application: Spray directly on whitefly colonies on leaf undersides.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 3-4 days.

prevention: Remove and burn virus-infected plants immediately — the virus has no cure
Plant maize or sorghum as border crop to reduce whitefly entry
Use reflective silver/aluminium mulch to repel whiteflies
Avoid planting in dry months when whitefly populations are highest

faqs.q: Can the infected plant recover?
faqs.a: No. Once a plant has the virus, it cannot recover. Remove it to protect other plants.

faqs.q: Is the virus in the soil?
faqs.a: No. The virus is carried only by whiteflies. Controlling whiteflies protects your field.

faqs.q: Can I save seeds from infected plants?
faqs.a: No. Do not save seeds from virus-infected plants as they may carry the virus.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: If more than 20% of plants are affected — the disease spreads fast through whiteflies.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
symptoms: White powdery coating on upper leaf surface
Powder also appears on stems and young fruit
Leaves turn yellow then brown and fall
Premature ripening and poor fruit quality
Affected parts look like dusted with flour
diseases.cause: Fungal infection by Erysiphe or Leveillula species. Unlike most fungi, it thrives in dry conditions.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Dry weather with moderate temperatures 20-27 degrees C, low humidity, poor air circulation

organic_remedies.name: Baking Soda Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 5g baking soda (meetha soda) plus 20ml neem oil plus 2 drops liquid soap in 1 litre of water.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on infected leaves in early morning. Changes pH on leaf surface which kills fungus.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 5-7 days for 3-4 weeks.

organic_remedies.name: Wood Ash Solution
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 100g wood ash in 5 litres of water. Let sit overnight. Strain through cloth before use.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on both leaf surfaces. The alkaline pH kills the fungus.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 7 days.

organic_remedies.name: Cow Urine Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Dilute fresh cow urine 1:10 with water.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on leaves. Avoid during flowering as smell may attract insects.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 7 days.

prevention: Maintain proper plant spacing — 45-60cm between plants
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilizers — they make soft tissue that fungi love
Water at the base of plants, never on leaves
Remove infected leaves and dispose off-field

faqs.q: Can I wash off the white powder?
faqs.a: You can wipe it off but the fungus will return unless you spray a treatment. The powder is the fungal spores — washing spreads them.

faqs.q: My grapes have white powder — is this powdery mildew?
faqs.a: Yes, grapes are very commonly affected. Apply baking soda spray or sulphur-based organic spray every 7 days.

faqs.q: Does rain help remove powdery mildew?
faqs.a: Surprisingly no — powdery mildew actually prefers dry conditions. Rain can temporarily wash spores off but the fungus quickly returns.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: If more than 40% of crop area is covered within two weeks despite treatment.

diseases.name: Root Rot / Fusarium Wilt
symptoms: Plant wilts suddenly during daytime even when soil has adequate moisture
Lower leaves turn yellow and drop
Dark brown or reddish-brown discolouration visible inside stem when cut lengthwise
Roots appear brown, black, and mushy instead of white and firm
Wilting does not recover even after evening watering
diseases.cause: Soil-borne fungi Fusarium oxysporum or Phytophthora. Enters through roots, then blocks water transport inside stem.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Waterlogged soil, poor drainage, heavy clay soils, soil pH below 5.5

organic_remedies.name: Trichoderma Soil Treatment
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 1kg Trichoderma viride powder with 100kg well-rotted farm yard manure (FYM). Moisten slightly and keep in shade for 15 days before use.
organic_remedies.application: Apply to top 6 inches of soil before planting. Or mix 10g in 1 litre water and drench around plant base.
organic_remedies.frequency: Once before planting as prevention. For active infection: drench every 15 days.

organic_remedies.name: Jeevamrut Drench
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 10 litres cow dung plus 10 litres cow urine plus 2kg jaggery plus 2kg pulse flour in 200 litres of water. Stir daily for 48 hours to ferment.
organic_remedies.application: Pour 500ml per plant directly at root zone in evening.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 15 days during growing season.

organic_remedies.name: Garlic-Ginger Extract
organic_remedies.preparation: Grind 100g garlic plus 100g ginger in 1 litre water. Strain through cloth.
organic_remedies.application: Pour 100ml diluted in 1 litre water per plant at root zone.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 7 days for 4 weeks.

prevention: Improve field drainage before planting — add sand or organic matter to heavy soils
Never overwater — let soil dry slightly between irrigations
Use raised beds or ridges in waterlogging-prone areas
Apply lime if soil pH is below 6.0 to neutralise acidity
Never replant the same crop family in soil where wilt occurred

faqs.q: My plant wilts during day but recovers at night — is this root rot?
faqs.a: Early stage yes — this is the classic sign of Fusarium wilt. Act immediately with Trichoderma drench before the plant loses the ability to recover at all.

faqs.q: Can I save an infected plant?
faqs.a: If caught early (recovers at night), Trichoderma drench can slow it. If the plant no longer recovers at night, remove it to protect the rest of the field.

faqs.q: Will the same disease come back next season?
faqs.a: Yes — Fusarium lives in soil for 10+ years. Rotate crops, add Trichoderma to soil before next planting, and improve drainage.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: Immediately — this disease spreads fast through soil and can destroy your entire crop within 2-3 weeks.

diseases.name: Aphid Infestation
symptoms: Clusters of tiny soft green, black, or yellow insects on stem tips and leaf undersides
New leaves curl and distort due to sap sucking
Sticky shiny coating (honeydew) on leaves
Black sooty mold growing on the honeydew
Plant growth slows; new growth is weak and distorted
diseases.cause: Infestation by Aphididae family insects. They suck plant sap and reproduce very quickly — one aphid can produce 80 offspring in a week.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Dry weather, warm temperatures 15-25 degrees C, excess nitrogen fertilization making soft new growth

organic_remedies.name: Neem Oil Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 50ml neem oil plus 5ml liquid soap in 5 litres of water. Shake vigorously before each use.
organic_remedies.application: Spray directly on aphid colonies especially on leaf undersides. Neem disrupts aphid reproduction.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 4-5 days for 3 consecutive applications. Then monitor weekly.

organic_remedies.name: Strong Water Jet
organic_remedies.preparation: No preparation needed — use a hose with strong pressure.
organic_remedies.application: Knock aphids off plants with strong jet of water in early morning. Aphids on ground cannot climb back easily.
organic_remedies.frequency: Daily for 5 days, then monitor.

organic_remedies.name: Garlic-Chilli Spray
organic_remedies.preparation: Grind 5 garlic cloves plus 2 green chillies in 1 litre of water. Filter through cloth.
organic_remedies.application: Spray on infected areas. The pungent smell repels aphids.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 3 days for 2 weeks.

prevention: Encourage natural predators — ladybirds, lacewings, and parasitic wasps eat aphids
Plant marigold or coriander near the crop as companion plants to attract predators
Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen — soft growth attracts aphids
Use yellow sticky traps for early detection before population explodes

faqs.q: There is black mold growing on my leaves — is this a different disease?
faqs.a: The black mold is sooty mold growing on aphid honeydew. Control the aphids first and the mold will disappear on its own.

faqs.q: The aphids keep coming back every week — why?
faqs.a: Aphid life cycle is very short. You need 3 consecutive sprays every 4-5 days to break the cycle. Single sprays are not enough.

faqs.q: I see ants on my plants wherever there are aphids — why?
faqs.a: Ants farm aphids for their honeydew and protect them from predators. Putting a sticky band on the stem base prevents ants from climbing up.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: If the infestation covers more than 50% of plants within one week despite spraying.

diseases.name: Nutrient Deficiency
symptoms: Uniform yellowing of older lower leaves first = Nitrogen deficiency
Yellowing between leaf veins while veins remain green = Magnesium or Iron deficiency
Purple or reddish tint on leaves = Phosphorus deficiency
Brown scorched leaf edges = Potassium deficiency
Yellowing of youngest top leaves = Iron or Zinc deficiency
diseases.cause: Lack of essential nutrients in soil, incorrect soil pH preventing nutrient uptake, overwatering washing away nutrients, compacted soil restricting root growth.
diseases.favourable_conditions: Sandy soil, acidic soil below pH 5.5, alkaline soil above pH 7.5, waterlogged soil

organic_remedies.name: Jeevamrut
organic_remedies.preparation: Mix 10 litres cow dung plus 10 litres cow urine plus 2kg jaggery plus 2kg pulse flour (besan) in 200 litres of water. Stir well. Cover and keep for 48 hours stirring once daily. Fermentation is complete when you see bubbles.
organic_remedies.application: Dilute 10 litres Jeevamrut in 100 litres water for soil drench. Or dilute 1:10 and spray on leaves as foliar feed.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 15 days throughout growing season. Addresses nitrogen, phosphorus, and micronutrient deficiency.

organic_remedies.name: Vermicompost Application
organic_remedies.preparation: Use finished vermicompost directly — should be dark brown, earthy smell, no visible worms.
organic_remedies.application: Apply 200-300g per plant around root zone and mix lightly into top soil. Water after application.
organic_remedies.frequency: Once a month throughout growing season.

organic_remedies.name: Banana Peel Compost Tea
organic_remedies.preparation: Soak 5-6 banana peels in 2 litres of water for 2 days.
organic_remedies.application: Pour the liquid around plant base — rich in potassium, good for fruiting stage.
organic_remedies.frequency: Every 2-3 weeks, especially during flowering and fruit development.

prevention: Get soil tested at KVK once a year — know your soil's actual nutrient levels
Maintain soil pH between 6.0 and 7.0 — most nutrients are unavailable outside this range
Add compost or FYM every season to improve soil organic matter
Use green manure crops like dhaincha or sesbania before the main crop

faqs.q: My lower leaves are yellow but upper leaves are green — what nutrient is missing?
faqs.a: This pattern (lower leaves yellow first) is classic Nitrogen deficiency. Apply Jeevamrut drench immediately — results visible in 5-7 days.

faqs.q: Leaves are yellow between the veins but veins are green — what is this?
faqs.a: This is Magnesium or Iron deficiency called interveinal chlorosis. Apply Jeevamrut foliar spray. Also check if soil is too acidic or alkaline.

faqs.q: I added a lot of FYM but still getting yellow leaves — why?
faqs.a: The problem may be soil pH. If pH is too low (acidic) or too high (alkaline), plants cannot absorb nutrients even if they are present. Get soil tested.

faqs.q: How quickly will the plants recover after I apply Jeevamrut?
faqs.a: For foliar spray, you should see improvement in new leaves within 7-10 days. Older yellow leaves will not turn green again, but new growth will be healthy.

diseases.when_to_consult_kvk: If yellowing spreads rapidly to the entire plant or does not improve within 2 weeks of treatment. Get a soil test done.
